moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Polish Opening, a quiet but ambitious sideline that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside. By advancing our b-pawn, we control the c5 and a5 squares, preparing to fianchetto our bishop on b2 where it will eye the long diagonal toward g7. This move is unconventional compared to 1.e4 or 1.d4, which makes it psychologically interesting—your opponent must think for themselves rather than follow well-memorized lines. The move fits a strategic style where we're willing to play slowly and positionally, letting Black choose their setup before we commit our center.

Black takes space in the center and follows classical opening principles by occupying the heart of the board. This move gives them flexibility and lets them build a solid position, though it does commit to a pawn structure that we can later challenge with moves like d4. By playing e5 here, Black is essentially saying they want to compete for control rather than let us build up slowly on the queenside.

This is the Bugayev Attack—we reinforce our queenside expansion and prevent Black from playing ...b5 to counterattack on the other wing. More importantly, a3 prepares b5 in many lines, and keeps our queenside ambitions alive while maintaining flexibility. This move reveals our plan: we're not just playing for space with b4, we're systematically advancing our queenside pawns to cramp Black's pieces and create imbalances. The move also subtly supports a future fianchetto on b2, as our b-pawn and bishop will control the long diagonal unopposed.
